exercises.title: Why a trainable bridge between frozen models
exercises.prompt: BLIP-2 connects a frozen ViT to a frozen LLM with a small trainable Q-Former (188M params bridging an 11B LLM). Explain why a trainable bottleneck between two frozen models beats end-to-end fine-tuning in cost and stability.
exercises.hint: Frozen encoder + frozen LLM = you only train the tiny bridge → cheap (few params), stable (can't catastrophically forget the LLM's language or the encoder's vision), reuses pretrained capability. End-to-end retrains huge models, risks forgetting, costs more.
exercises.solution:
**The setup:** BLIP-2 keeps the **vision encoder frozen** and the **LLM frozen**, and trains only a small **Q-Former** (188M params) between them — connecting an 11B LLM to a ViT-g/14 with a bridge ~1.7% the size of the LLM alone.

**Why a trainable bottleneck beats end-to-end fine-tuning:**
1. **Cost:** you train only the **tiny bridge** (188M params), not the 11B LLM or the billion-param vision encoder. Far less compute, memory, and data — you can train it on modest hardware, and the expensive pretrained models are reused as-is.
2. **Stability / no catastrophic forgetting:** the LLM's **language ability** and the encoder's **visual features** are the product of enormous pretraining. Fine-tuning them end-to-end on a comparatively tiny multimodal dataset risks **catastrophically forgetting** that capability (the LLM gets worse at language, the encoder at vision) and is **unstable** (large models, small data, tricky optimization). **Freezing both** *guarantees* their capabilities are preserved — the bridge can only learn to **translate** between them, never degrade them.
3. **Reuse / modularity:** you can **swap in different frozen LLMs or encoders** and just retrain the small bridge, rather than re-doing a massive end-to-end run. The pretrained investments are leveraged, not redone.
4. **Data efficiency:** a small bridge needs far less aligned image-text data to train than fine-tuning billions of parameters.
The tradeoff: a frozen-everything design may leave some quality on the table vs full fine-tuning (the LLM never adapts its weights to vision), but the **cost/stability/reuse** win was decisive — and **every adapter-based VLM through 2026** (MiniGPT-4, InstructBLIP, and LLaVA's cousins) descends from this 'small trainable bridge between frozen towers' pattern. (LLaVA later showed an even simpler bridge — an MLP — often suffices, Lesson 5.)

exercises.title: Learnable queries and cross-attention
exercises.prompt: Implement the Q-Former's core mechanism: a fixed set of learnable query vectors that attend to a frozen ViT's image features via cross-attention, producing a fixed number of visual tokens for the LLM. Explain why 32 learnable queries give a constant token budget regardless of image resolution, and the two-stage training.
exercises.hint: 32 query vectors cross-attend (queries=Q, image features=K,V) → 32 output vectors → fed to LLM. Fixed 32 outputs no matter how many patches → constant token budget. Stage 1: representation learning (ITC+ITM+ITG). Stage 2: generative (LM loss, frozen decoder).
exercises.solution:
```python
import numpy as np

def cross_attention(queries, img_features, Wq, Wk, Wv):
    """queries: (K, d) learnable; img_features: (N_patches, d_v) from frozen ViT."""
    Q = queries @ Wq                       # (K, d)
    Kk = img_features @ Wk                  # (N, d)
    V = img_features @ Wv                   # (N, d)
    scores = (Q @ Kk.T) / np.sqrt(Q.shape[-1])   # (K, N): each query over all patches
    attn = softmax(scores, axis=-1)
    return attn @ V                          # (K, d): K visual tokens, regardless of N

# 32 learnable queries -> 32 output tokens, ALWAYS
queries = np.random.randn(32, 768)          # the only thing (plus W's) that's trained
```

**The mechanism:** the Q-Former holds **32 learnable query vectors**. They **cross-attend** to the frozen ViT's image patch features — the **queries are Q**, the **image features are K and V**. Each query attends over **all** image patches and extracts a weighted summary, producing **32 output vectors** that are then projected into the LLM's input stream as visual tokens. The queries are **learned** to extract the most useful visual information for downstream language tasks (and through the cross-attention, they're conditioned on text in stage 1).

**Why 32 queries = constant token budget:** the number of output tokens equals the **number of queries (32)**, **not the number of image patches**. A 224px image (196 patches) and a 1024px image (thousands of patches) **both produce exactly 32 visual tokens** — the Q-Former **compresses any number of patches into a fixed 32-token bottleneck**. This is the Q-Former's headline advantage: a **small, constant visual-token cost** to the LLM regardless of resolution (vs LLaVA's MLP, which passes *all* patch tokens through, scaling with resolution). It keeps the LLM's context cheap — the 'token-budget win' the lesson notes.

**The two-stage training:**
- **Stage 1 — representation learning** (Q-Former + frozen ViT, no LLM yet): train the queries to extract good visual features aligned with text using **three objectives** — **ITC** (Image-Text Contrastive, CLIP-style alignment), **ITM** (Image-Text Matching, a binary 'do they match?' classifier with hard negatives), and **ITG** (Image-grounded Text Generation, generate the caption from the queries). Different attention masks select which objective the queries serve. This teaches the queries to **distill the image into language-relevant features**.
- **Stage 2 — generative pretraining** (Q-Former + frozen LLM): connect the Q-Former's outputs to the **frozen LLM** and train with the **LM loss** (generate text conditioned on the visual tokens) so the queries learn to produce features the **specific LLM** can consume. Only the Q-Former (and a projection) updates; the LLM stays frozen.
The split — first learn *good visual representations*, then learn to *speak to the LLM* — is what lets a tiny bridge connect two frozen giants. Compared to LLaVA's MLP projector: **Q-Former compresses to a fixed token budget and learns richer features but is more complex; the MLP is simpler and passes all tokens (Lesson 5).**

exercises.title: Q-Former vs MLP projector: why simpler won
exercises.prompt: BLIP-2's Q-Former is elegant but the community largely moved to LLaVA's simple MLP projector. Compare the two on token budget, training complexity, information preservation, and scaling. Explain when Q-Former still wins, why 'simpler won' for most VLMs, and what this says about the bitter-lesson dynamics in multimodal design.
exercises.hint: Q-Former: fixed token budget (good for cost/long video), richer features, but complex 2-stage training and an information bottleneck. MLP: passes all patch tokens (scales tokens with resolution), trivial to train, preserves all info, scales with data/compute. Simpler+more tokens+more data won (bitter lesson). Q-Former wins when token budget is the binding constraint.
exercises.solution:
**Comparison:**
| | **Q-Former (BLIP-2)** | **MLP projector (LLaVA)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Token budget** | **fixed (e.g. 32)** regardless of resolution — cheap, constant | **all patch tokens** — scales with resolution (expensive at high-res) |
| **Training complexity** | **complex**: 2 stages, 3 objectives (ITC/ITM/ITG), learnable queries | **trivial**: a 2-layer MLP, single-stage alignment + instruction tuning |
| **Information preservation** | **bottleneck**: 32 queries compress the image → some detail lost | **lossless**: every patch token reaches the LLM → full detail |
| **Scaling behavior** | the bottleneck can **cap** how much visual detail flows; harder to scale | scales cleanly with **resolution, data, compute** — more tokens, more info |
| **Where it shines** | token-constrained settings (long video, many images, tight context) | high-detail single images, OCR, charts; ease of training/iteration |

**When Q-Former still wins:** when the **visual-token budget is the binding constraint** — e.g. **long video** (hundreds of frames × all patch tokens would explode the context), **many-image** inputs, or deployment with a small context window. A fixed-token bottleneck per image/frame keeps the sequence manageable, which is why **token-compression ideas persist** (pooling, resamplers) in video and long-context VLMs (Lessons 8, 17, 18). The Perceiver resampler (Flamingo, Lesson 4) is the same idea.

**Why 'simpler won' for most VLMs:** LLaVA showed that a **2-layer MLP + token concatenation**, trained on **GPT-4-generated instruction data**, **matched or beat** the Q-Former on standard single-image benchmarks — with **far less complexity**. The reasons:
1. **The MLP preserves all visual information** (no bottleneck), so detail-heavy tasks (OCR, charts, fine-grained VQA) benefit — and the LLM is powerful enough to use the raw patch tokens.
2. **It's trivial to train and iterate** — no 3-objective 2-stage curriculum — so the community could move fast, and **most of the quality came from data and the LLM**, not the connector.
3. **It scales cleanly:** add resolution (more tokens), better encoders, more data — no architectural bottleneck to redesign.

**The bitter-lesson dynamics:** this is a textbook **'bitter lesson'** (Sutton) in multimodal design — **clever, hand-designed structure (the Q-Former's learnable-query bottleneck) lost to a simpler, more general approach (MLP + more tokens + more data + a bigger LLM) that scales with compute and data.** The ablation literature (Lesson 7) confirms it: **the connector architecture matters less than the encoder and far less than the data mixture.** The Q-Former's elegance (fixed token budget, learned compression) was a real engineering win *for a specific constraint*, but when **scale removes that constraint** (you can afford the tokens, you have lots of data), the **simpler, less-opinionated bridge wins** because it doesn't throw away information and it rides the scaling curve. The lasting lesson: in multimodal as in LLMs, **don't over-engineer the connector — invest in the encoder, the data, and scale**, and reach for compression bottlenecks (Q-Former-style) **only when the token budget genuinely forces your hand** (long video, many images). Simpler + more data + more scale is the recurring winner.
